feedback.text: Thank you for taking the time to read feedback on these issues. I have two major concerns related online safety and fairness that I hope can be taken into account when working on this legislation. First, the increasing power wielded by American payment processors such as Visa, Master Card and PayPal. The monopoly these companies have has given them unprecedented power over many digital storefronts, such as Steam and Itch or other digital storefronts such as Patreon. The payment processors have recently struck many of these digital storefronts with heavy restrictions on the kind of material people can buy or monetarily support on them, with many of these restrictions happening to adult content or works that are completely free of such content but heavily feature LGBT-related themes. The products getting hit do not feature anything illegal, but are nonetheless punished due to the payment processors' personal distaste for such content. Allowing these companies this level of power over what people can buy with their own money is a massive risk to consumer rights and sets a dangerous precedent for even more harmful restrictions in the future. Considering that President Trump is already attacking Brazil over their local payment processor Pix, something many people believe to be due to lobbying from these very same companies, it's not hard to imagine similar attacks happening against EU-developed payment processors in the future. To protect EU-citizens' customer rights, I believe heavy regulation is needed to stop this kind of overreach from payment processors if they wish to operate within the EU. Second, I have concerns about how some entities within the EU are suggesting using personal info as a way to access online spaces that feature any kind of adult content. I have nothing but sympathy to people's concerns about minors being exposed to content not meant for them, but making personal info the requirement for accessing it is a massive cyber security risk. This kind of info can be extremely harmful if it ends up in incorrect hands, which can and does happen due to hacking or malicious actions from third parties that would be hosting said data. Digital Fairness Act seeks to specifically ensure that EU-citizens' personal info is not used to against them in malicious ways and I don't believe forcing people to put their personal info to risk to access regular adult spaces would be in the spirit of the Act. EU must be able to guarantee genuine, unbreakable safety for this info. 99,99% is not safe enough. If this cannot be reached, then these kinds of measures will just put EU citizens at unnecessary risk. I think it's important to keep in mind that many online spaces that host adult content are also regular social media spaces one can use without ever truly witnessing anything that would be classified as adult material. People who aren't using these spaces for anything unfit for minors would also be putting themselves at a risk just to use them. To say nothing of the fact that there's now a clear link between their online presence and personal info that can be used to identify them offline. This makes a huge risk to anyone using these social media sites to express thoughts and opinions that would put them in danger in their daily life. Even outside social media, many sites could be facing issues, as can be seen with the consequences of the recently implemented Online Safety Act in the UK. The Act has restricted children's access to sites dealing with domestic violence, sex education and even Wikipedia is currently in a struggle to be allowed to stay available to UK citizens. Are we going to be going to follow this path down as well? All these sites certainly contain content that can be upsetting to minors but does that make them ban-worthy? I believe instead of heavily restricting online spaces and access to them, the EU should focus on greater parental control systems for these spaces and to encourage parents to use them.
